data.title: Has Venture Global (VG) Pulled Back Enough To Consider The Recent Valuation Gap?
data.description:
• Wondering if Venture Global at US$11.46 is still good value after its recent run, or if most of the easy upside is already priced in.
  • The stock has seen a mixed patch recently, with an 11.6% decline over the last 7 days and a 27.5% decline over the last 30 days, yet it still shows a 62.8% gain year to date and a 33.8% return over the last year.
  • These moves have put valuation back in focus for many investors, especially as sentiment can shift quickly when expectations change. Even without headline grabbing announcements, the recent volatility makes it more important to understand what the current price implies about future prospects.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives Venture Global a value score of 5/6. This article will unpack that score using several valuation approaches, before finishing with a broader way to think about what it really means for long term investors.

Venture Global delivered 33.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Oil and Gas industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Venture Global Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars, aiming to estimate what the entire business could be worth right now.

For Venture Global, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $11,614.68 million, and analyst inputs plus Simply Wall St extrapolations suggest free cash flow remains negative in the near term, with projections such as $6,139.80 million and $6,118.67 million of outflows in 2026 and 2027, then shifting to inflows including $3,365.50 million in 2030.

Discounting these annual figures back to today and adding them together gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $27.75 per share. Compared with the current share price of $11.46, this DCF output implies the stock trades at a 58.7% discount to that estimate. On these assumptions, the model indicates that Venture Global may be materially undervalued relative to this DCF estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Venture Global is undervalued by 58.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Venture Global.

Approach 2: Venture Global Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support it. It helps you see how much the market is willing to pay today for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E usually reflects how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk often lead to a lower P/E.

Venture Global currently trades on a P/E of 12.59x. That is below both the Oil and Gas industry average P/E of 14.88x and a broader peer group average of 29.73x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Venture Global is 13.19x, which is a proprietary estimate of what its P/E might be given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk profile.

The Fair Ratio can be more useful than a simple industry or peer comparison because it is tailored to Venture Global’s own characteristics, rather than assuming all companies in the same sector deserve similar multiples. Compared with the current P/E of 12.59x, the Fair Ratio of 13.19x suggests the shares screen as slightly undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: There's A Lot To Like About Alfa Laval's (STO:ALFA) Upcoming kr09.00 Dividend
data.description:
Alfa Laval AB (publ) (STO:ALFA) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 3 days. The ex-dividend date generally occurs two days before the record date, which is the day on which shareholders need to be on the company's books in order to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Therefore, if you purchase Alfa Laval's shares on or after the 23rd of April, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 29th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be kr09.00 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of kr9.00 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Alfa Laval has a trailing yield of 1.6% on the current share price of kr0573.00. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Fortunately Alfa Laval's payout ratio is modest, at just 45% of profit. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Alfa Laval generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 54% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's positive to see that Alfa Laval's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

View our latest analysis for Alfa Laval

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. Fortunately for readers, Alfa Laval's earnings per share have been growing at 19% a year for the past five years. Alfa Laval has an average payout ratio which suggests a balance between growing earnings and rewarding shareholders. This is a reasonable combination that could hint at some further dividend increases in the future.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. In the past 10 years, Alfa Laval has increased its dividend at approximately 7.8% a year on average. We're glad to see dividends rising alongside earnings over a number of years, which may be a sign the company intends to share the growth with shareholders.

To Sum It Up

Should investors buy Alfa Laval for the upcoming dividend? From a dividend perspective, we're encouraged to see that earnings per share have been growing, the company is paying out less than half of its earnings, and a bit over half its free cash flow. It's a promising combination that should mark this company worthy of closer attention.

data.title: Here's What We Like About Bufab's (STO:BUFAB) Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
Bufab AB (publ) (STO:BUFAB) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date is usually set to be two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date on which you must be present on the company's books as a shareholder in order to receive the dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Accordingly, Bufab investors that purchase the stock on or after the 24th of April will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 30th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be kr01.30 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of kr1.30 to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Bufab stock has a trailing yield of around 1.1% on the current share price of kr0120.20. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. That's why it's good to see Bufab paying out a modest 39% of its earnings. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. It paid out 24% of its free cash flow as dividends last year, which is conservatively low.

It's positive to see that Bufab's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. For this reason, we're glad to see Bufab's earnings per share have risen 15% per annum over the last five years. The company has managed to grow earnings at a rapid rate, while reinvesting most of the profits within the business. Fast-growing businesses that are reinvesting heavily are enticing from a dividend perspective, especially since they can often increase the payout ratio later.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Bufab has delivered 14% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. Both per-share earnings and dividends have both been growing rapidly in recent times, which is great to see.

The Bottom Line

Is Bufab worth buying for its dividend? It's great that Bufab is growing earnings per share while simultaneously paying out a low percentage of both its earnings and cash flow. It's disappointing to see the dividend has been cut at least once in the past, but as things stand now, the low payout ratio suggests a conservative approach to dividends, which we like. data.title: Income Investors Should Know That Aker ASA (OB:AKER) Goes Ex-Dividend Soon
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Aker ASA (OB:AKER) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next three days. The ex-dividend date is usually set to be two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date on which you must be present on the company's books as a shareholder in order to receive the dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Thus, you can purchase Aker's shares before the 23rd of April in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 5th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is kr029.00 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of kr53.00 per share to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Aker has a trailing yield of 5.4% on the current share price of kr01026.00. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Aker's dividend is reliable and sustainable. As a result, readers should always check whether Aker has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. Aker paid out more than half (53%) of its earnings last year, which is a regular payout ratio for most companies. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Over the last year, it paid out dividends equivalent to 272% of what it generated in free cash flow, a disturbingly high percentage. Unless there were something in the business we're not grasping, this could signal a risk that the dividend may have to be cut in the future.

Aker paid out less in dividends than it reported in profits, but unfortunately it didn't generate enough cash to cover the dividend. Cash is king, as they say, and were Aker to repeatedly pay dividends that aren't well covered by cashflow, we would consider this a warning sign.

View our latest analysis for Aker

Click here to see how much of its profit Aker paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. That's why it's comforting to see Aker's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 35% per annum for the past five years. Earnings have been growing quickly, but we're concerned dividend payments consumed most of the company's cash flow over the past year.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. Aker has delivered 19% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. It's great to see earnings per share growing rapidly over several years, and dividends per share growing right along with it.

Final Takeaway

Is Aker an attractive dividend stock, or better left on the shelf? It's good to see that earnings per share are growing and that the company's payout ratio is within a normal range for most businesses. However we're somewhat concerned that it paid out 272% of its cashflow, which is uncomfortably high. Overall, it's hard to get excited about Aker from a dividend perspective.

However if you're still interested in Aker as a potential investment, you should definitely consider some of the risks involved with Aker. For instance, we've identified 4 warning signs for Aker (2 are a bit concerning) you should be aware of.
